Welcome back to Innovative eLearning Ideas! Tracking changes in educational policies, new research, or EdTech updates used to mean endless Googling. But at Google recently announced a massive shift in Information Agents in Search. Instead of just returning links, these agents act as your persistent research assistants, constantly monitoring the web in the background and sending you synthesized updates when meaningful changes happen.

𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗦𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗰𝗵 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀 𝗠𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗘𝗱𝘂𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘀:

🎯 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗻𝘂𝗼𝘂𝘀 𝗠𝗼𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗼𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴: Your agent intelligently scans blogs, news sites, social posts, and fresh data sources to track specific topics continuously.

⏳ 𝗦𝘆𝗻𝘁𝗵𝗲𝘀𝗶𝘇𝗲𝗱 𝗨𝗽𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗲𝘀: Instead of manually comparing new articles, the agent delivers an intelligent, synthesized summary directly to you. The agent can explain why changes matter and help you take action.

🧠𝗠𝘂𝗹𝘁𝗶-𝗧𝗮𝘀𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝘁 𝗦𝗰𝗮𝗹𝗲: You can spin up multiple agents simultaneously to track different subjects without needing to keep search tabs open.

𝗛𝗼𝘄 𝘁𝗼 𝗘𝘅𝗲𝗰𝘂𝘁𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗸𝗳𝗹𝗼𝘄:

🗣️ 𝗧𝗵𝗲 "𝗣𝗼𝗹𝗶𝗰𝘆 𝗧𝗿𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗲𝗿" (𝗔𝗱𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻/𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽):Stay ahead of compliance and district policy shifts without lifting a finger.
🚨 Example Workflow: Set up an information agent in Google Search to track new educational guidelines. The agent works around the clock, notifying you only when new official guidelines or major news are published.

📖 𝗧𝗵𝗲 "𝗖𝘂𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗼𝗿" (𝗧𝗲𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗲𝗿𝘀/𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗳𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗿𝘀):Bring the freshest real-world data into your lesson plans.
🚨 Example Workflow: Teach biology? Spin up an agent to monitor breakthroughs in your subject. The agent will alert you when a new study drops, summarizing the key findings so you can share it with your students the next morning.

𝗥𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗿: The internet is too big for any single teacher to monitor manually.

Caption:
🧠 ¿Qué son los WORLD MODELS para agentes de IA?

Qwen acaba de lanzar Qwen-AgentWorld, un modelo que simula entornos completos para que los agentes de IA aprendan sin tocar el mundo real.

👉 Deslizá para entender cómo funciona:

1️⃣ Un World Model predice qué va a pasar en un entorno antes de que pase. Es como la "imaginación" de una IA.

2️⃣ El agente ejecuta acciones en un simulador (no en el mundo real). Aprende por prueba y error sin romper nada.

3️⃣ Qwen-AgentWorld simula 7 dominios distintos: web, código, archivos, APIs, y más.

4️⃣ Resultado: agentes más inteligentes, entrenados más rápido, con menos datos reales.

5️⃣ Esto cambia todo: ya no necesitás miles de horas de interacción real para entrenar un agente. El modelo imagina los escenarios.

¿Te imaginás un agente que aprenda a usar cualquier app nueva en minutos? Eso es lo que viene. 🚀

Caption:
Stop treating AI like a fancy chat box and start treating it like a strategic partner. 🤖

The real breakthrough in productivity isn't found in mastering the "perfect prompt." Instead, it’s found in building repeatable workflows and persistent AI agents that can manage complex, multi-step business processes.

The true value of AI lies in augmentation, not replacement. When we stop viewing technology as a competitor and start seeing it as a collaborator, we unlock a level of scale that was previously impossible. 

To do this effectively, we must define a clear division of labor:
✅ Let AI handle the complex reasoning.
✅ Let automation take over repetitive execution.
✅ Reserve high-level strategy and genuine human connection for your team.

By letting technology handle the heavy lifting of data and routine tasks, you free your people to do what they do best—lead, innovate, and connect. This is how we move from simply "using AI" to truly evolving alongside it.

Caption:
Anthropic just put a spy in your Slack. A smart one.

Claude Tag is now sitting in your workspace, reading every message, learning your company's language, your internal logic, your unwritten rules. It's always on. It's always listening.

Most people will use it to summarize threads and draft replies. Fine. That's the surface layer.

But here's what actually matters: Anthropic is building a memory system for every enterprise on the planet. Every Slack workspace that adopts this becomes a training ground for organizational context. Claude doesn't just know what GPT-4 knows. It knows *your* company.

That's the moat they're building. Not the model. The context.

For builders, this is the signal: the next wave of valuable AI products isn't about raw intelligence. It's about who has the deepest context. Your internal docs. Your customer history. Your team's reasoning patterns.

If you're building for B2B, stop thinking about features. Start thinking about what context you can capture that nobody else can touch.

The companies that win won't have the best AI. They'll have AI that knows the most about their specific world.

Claude Tag is Anthropic's bet that the best place to capture that knowledge is where work actually happens. Slack, every day, all day.

Hard to argue with that logic.

Question for builders: are you designing your products to get smarter over time through user context — or are you just wrapping an API?

Caption:
Prompt engineering improves the first response. Loop engineering improves the final outcome.

As AI coding agents take on more complex software tasks, a strong prompt is no longer enough.

Real projects require agents to work through a structured cycle:

Plan → Search → Modify → Verify → Repair → Summarise

This is the idea behind Loop Engineering: designing AI workflows where agents use tests, logs, compiler errors, code reviews, and human feedback to continuously improve their work.

A well-designed loop helps teams:

• Reduce unverified AI-generated code
• Make smaller, reversible changes
• Detect wrong assumptions early
• Improve reliability through continuous validation
• Keep humans in control of product and architectural decisions

The next generation of AI-enabled teams will not compete only on who writes better prompts.

They will compete on who designs better loops.

Build systems, not just prompts.

Caption:
Voice agents are about to feel different.

Nvidia Nemotron ASR Streaming is the new listening layer for any ai voice agent stack. Real time transcription with cache aware FastConformer RNNT architecture, which means it does not waste cycles re-listening to audio it has already processed.

The result is lower latency, cleaner transcripts, and a voice product that feels like a conversation instead of a walkie talkie.

Two variants ship out of the box.

English only for tight latency on en US workloads.

Multilingual for 40 language locales with automatic language detection in a single checkpoint. That retires the per language ASR routing layer most global support teams have been stitching together for years.

Why this matters right now.

ASR latency compounds through every step of a voice agent. Planner, action layer, text to speech, transport. Shave time off the listening layer and the entire interaction speeds up.

Multilingual coverage is no longer optional. The multilingual variant collapses a routing problem into a model call.

Deployment is a NIM container, not a six month infrastructure project.

What a serious rollout looks like.

Pick the variant for your language coverage.

Smoke test on real production audio.

Measure microphone to first token latency, not just model latency.

Wire the transcript into whatever consumes it next. Planner, summarizer, CRM, downstream LLM.

Monitor word error rate, latency percentiles, and task success. Optimize against task success.

Teams that anchor their voice strategy on a streaming first ASR engine this year will have a head start.

Voice is finally ready to feel real.

Caption:
A AWS lançou o Lambda MicroVMs, recurso baseado em Firecracker que permite executar ambientes Linux isolados por até 8 horas, com preservação de estado, suspensão e retomada sob demanda. A novidade amplia o uso do Lambda para tarefas mais longas, como execução de código não confiável, análise de vulnerabilidades, workloads de CI/CD, sessões interativas e agentes de inteligência artificial.

O recurso oferece uma alternativa serverless para ambientes temporários e isolados, mas sua adoção exige atenção a permissões, rede, IAM, logs e controle de acesso, especialmente quando usado com agentes de IA capazes de executar comandos e acessar ferramentas corporativas. No lançamento, o Lambda MicroVMs está disponível em regiões selecionadas da AWS e na arquitetura ARM64 com Graviton.

Para ler a notícia completa acesse:

Caption:
☸️ Most ML engineers avoid Kubernetes because it looks intimidating.

But avoiding it doesn't make it go away, it just makes you dependent on someone else who understands it.

Here's Kubernetes for ML in 5 minutes 👇

Docker runs one container.

Kubernetes runs hundreds of containers reliably, with scaling, self-healing, and automation built in.

Why ML workloads need K8s:

⚡ GPU-intensive → Resource requests & limits prevent resource starvation

📈 Traffic spikes → Horizontal Pod Autoscaler scales automatically

💾 Persistent storage → Volumes survive pod restarts

⏰ Scheduled retraining → CronJobs automate recurring workflows

The 4 Kubernetes objects that cover most ML deployments:

• Pod → Runs your container
• Deployment → Manages replicas & updates
• Service → Stable endpoint for your app
• Ingress → Routes external traffic

Flow:

Ingress → Service → Deployment → Pods

One lesson most tutorials skip:

Always define resource requests and limits.

Without them:
❌ Autoscaling becomes unreliable
❌ One workload can consume an entire node
❌ Performance becomes unpredictable

And then there's Helm.

Instead of managing dozens of YAML files, Helm lets you template once and deploy consistently across environments.

You don't need to master Kubernetes overnight.

Start with managed platforms like GKE, EKS, or AKS and focus on your ML workloads first.

What's holding your team back from Kubernetes adoption, complexity, cost, or time to learn?

🎓 Want hands-on experience with Kubernetes, DevOps, MLOps, AIOps, and SRE on AWS?

Join the 28-Week Live Bootcamp:

https://livingdevops.com/courses/28-week-real-world-aws-devops-bootcamp-with-kubernetes-devsecops-sre/

Understanding AWS Global Infrastructure is essential for building applications that are secure, scalable, highly available, and fast for users worldwide.

𝗜𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝘃𝗶𝘀𝘂𝗮𝗹 𝗴𝘂𝗶𝗱𝗲, 𝘄𝗲 𝗯𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗸 𝗱𝗼𝘄𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗔𝗪𝗦 𝗶𝗻𝗳𝗿𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗼𝗻𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀:

✅ 𝗥𝗲𝗴𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 – Geographic AWS locations such as Mumbai, Hyderabad, Virginia, and London.
✅ 𝗔𝘃𝗮𝗶𝗹𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗭𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘀 (𝗔𝗭𝘀) – Isolated data centers inside a Region designed for high availability.
✅ 𝗘𝗱𝗴𝗲 𝗟𝗼𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 – Help deliver cached content faster using services like CloudFront.
✅ 𝗟𝗼𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝗭𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘀 – Bring compute and storage closer to metro cities for low-latency workloads.
✅ 𝗪𝗮𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗲𝗻𝗴𝘁𝗵 𝗭𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘀 – Support ultra-low latency applications over 5G networks.
✅ 𝗔𝗪𝗦 𝗢𝘂𝘁𝗽𝗼𝘀𝘁𝘀 – Extend AWS infrastructure and services into your on-premises data center.

𝗔 𝘀𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹-𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗹𝗱 𝗮𝗿𝗰𝗵𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝗶𝗻𝗰𝗹𝘂𝗱𝗲:

➡️Users → CloudFront → Application Load Balancer → EC2 across Multiple AZs → RDS Multi-AZ → S3

This design improves:

🔹 Application availability
🔹 Fault tolerance
🔹 Disaster recovery readiness
🔹 Performance and low latency
🔹 Scalability for growing workloads

𝗙𝗼𝗿 𝗰𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱 𝗹𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗲𝗿𝘀, 𝗿𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗸𝗲𝘆 𝗽𝗼𝗶𝗻𝘁:

Caption:
🚀 Start With Inventory, Not Infrastructure: Every cloud migration starts the same way:

Management: "How long will it take?"

Engineer: "First, we need to know what exists."

Management: "Isn't it just the server?" 😅

That’s how migrations go wrong.

Before AWS, GCP, Azure, Kubernetes, or Terraform, ask one question:

👉 What are we actually migrating?

Not just servers. Also:

✅ Apps

✅ Domains and DNS

✅ Databases

✅ Files and storage

✅ Cron jobs

✅ Env vars

✅ Third-party APIs

✅ SSL certs

✅ CI/CD pipelines

✅ Monitoring

✅ Backups

Because downtime usually happens when someone says:

"Oh... I forgot about that webhook."

Then:

🔥 Payments fail

🔥 Emails stop

🔥 Jobs break

🔥 Certs expire

🔥 Support tickets spike

The problem wasn’t the cloud. The problem was the missing inventory.

📋 Inventory first.

☁️ Infrastructure second.

Caption:
📅 Dia 07 de 30 Desafio de dados Databricks Professional

✨ Managed vs. External Tables: Onde seu dado realmente mora?

​Fechando a primeira semana do desafio com chave de ouro! Hoje o foco foi entender o impacto físico e o ciclo de vida dos dados no S3/ADLS. A diferença entre uma tabela Managed e uma External vai muito além da sintaxe do código — ela define quem é o dono do dado e o que acontece quando você roda um comando fatal.

​📌 Managed Tables (Tabelas Gerenciadas)
​O conceito: O Databricks gerencia tanto os metadados no Unity Catalog quanto os arquivos físicos no cloud storage.

​O perigo: Se você executar um DROP TABLE, o Unity Catalog apaga o registro da tabela E deleta permanentemente os arquivos físicos no S3/ADLS.

​Quando usar: Ideal para tabelas onde o Databricks tem controle total do ciclo de vida (como nas camadas Silver e Gold internas).

​📌 External Tables (Tabelas Externas)
​O conceito: Você define um caminho específico via LOCATION. O Databricks passa a gerenciar apenas os metadados.
​A segurança: Se você rodar um DROP TABLE, apenas o ponteiro/registro no Unity Catalog é apagado. Os seus arquivos físicos continuam intactos no storage.
​Quando usar: Essencial quando outras ferramentas externas precisam acessar os mesmos arquivos ou quando você precisa garantir que o dado bruto jamais será deletado por acidente.

​✨ Reflexão Diária
Entender o ciclo de vida físico do dado evita aquele frio na barriga clássico de sumir com Terabytes de produção por engano. Uma boa governança começa sabendo exatamente quem manda no ciclo de vida da sua infraestrutura.

Caption:
Your Delta Live Tables pipeline was doing completely fine… until it suddenly wasn’t. 🤯

We’ve all been there. You deploy a beautiful DLT pipeline in Databricks. It runs flawlessly on your test datasets. It even handles early production data like a charm.

But then, real-world data scaling kicks in. Data volume spikes, schema drift happens, or an unoptimized stateful stream bogs down the entire engine. Suddenly, your "automated" pipeline requires constant manual restarts. 📉

It’s not bad luck; it’s a design bottleneck.

To build resilient data infrastructure, you have to look under the hood of how DLT handles schema evolution, memory management, and data quality rules under heavy load.

Want to know the exact reasons these pipelines fail and the architectural fixes to stop it from happening again?

Caption:
Your AI agents don’t need perfect data. They need context. 🧠💻

Trying to build a flawless data warehouse is a quick way to burn out your data engineering team. Real ground truth shifts constantly, and column-level alerts quickly turn into noisy pipelines nobody pays attention to.

The solution? Risk-Based Data Quality Tiering. 🎯

By categorizing your data from Tier 0 (regulatory and revenue-critical tables backed by strict code-based contracts) to Tier 3 (one-off scratch tables), you protect what matters most without slowing down innovation.

🛑 The biggest trap? Tier creep. If everything is labeled Tier 0, nothing is.

Check out the link in our bio to read the framework modern data teams are using to scale their infrastructure for AI without drowning in paperwork.

Caption:
Dev Tip #4: MongoDB vs MySQL vs PostgreSQL — which one? 🗄️

Here's the quick guide:

MONGODB
→ NoSQL, flexible schema
→ Best for: unstructured data, fast prototyping, real-time apps

POSTGRESQL
→ Relational, ACID compliant
→ Best for: complex queries, data relationships, financial apps

MYSQL
→ Relational, widely supported
→ Best for: simple apps, shared hosting environments

Rule: If you're unsure, start with PostgreSQL. It'll scale with you.

A strong Technical SEO strategy ensures your website is fast, accessible, and optimized for both users and search engines.

✅ Website Speed Optimization

Caption:
If you're chasing trends, you're already late.

One of the biggest mistakes brands make in influencer marketing is believing that going viral means jumping on every trend.

It doesn't.

By the time a trend is identified, approved, briefed, produced, and published, the audience has often already moved on.

That's why so many trend-based campaigns fail. 
Not because the content is bad. 
Not because the creators are wrong. 
Because the timing is.

The brands that consistently generate results don't chase trends.

They identify the right trends early, evaluate whether they actually fit their audience, and activate the right creators before the opportunity disappears.

And there's another mistake many brands make: 
Assuming every viral trend is relevant to their business.

A trend with millions of views means nothing if it doesn't align with your audience, your product, or your brand positioning.

In influencer marketing, relevance almost always beats reach.

The best-performing campaigns happen when the right trend, the right creator, and the right audience meet at the right moment.

Virality isn't random. 
It's built.

What's the biggest trend-jacking mistake you've seen brands make? 
Let us know in the comments.

And if you're a brand looking to build influencer marketing strategies that go beyond simply chasing trends, contact our team through the link in bio. ⬆️

#InfluencerMarketing #TrendMarketing #CreatorMarketing

day got ready fast and got an Uber. I’m very Extroverted so I started talking about a lot of things to the driver and he asked me what my hobbies were and I told him that I write a lot and was planning to publish a book but was too expensive.

He then proceeded to guide me to a self publishing process where I found out was less than what I would have spent in normal publishing, up until this day l am grateful for the uber driver because of him I published my first poetry book in 2020. 😆🙌🏻

Not many people know of the book yet because I stopped sharing it but I can say I’m proud of how far I’ve come and my writing has improved immensely since. I hope one day I can get people’s input on my books and I’m hopefully tagged or shared with don’t worry I don’t mind criticism. 😌☺️

Caption:
The workflow ran flawlessly for 47 days. 14,000+ successful executions. Zero issues.

On day 48, at 3:42 AM, a payment gateway returned a 503 instead of a 200.

Without error handling: the entire pipeline crashed silently. No Slack alert. No retry. No fallback. Nobody noticed for 72 hours. 4,200 customer invoices were never processed. The team found out from a customer complaint — not from their own monitoring.

With error handling: the node retried 3 times with exponential backoff (2s, 4s, 8s). On the third failure, it triggered a fallback path that routed to a backup payment processor. Simultaneously, a Slack alert hit the on-call channel in 12 seconds. Failed records were queued in a dead letter queue for manual review. Total customer impact: zero.

5 error handling patterns every production workflow needs:

1. Retry with exponential backoff — 73% of transient API failures self-resolve within 3 attempts
2. Fallback/alternative paths — if the primary service is down, route to a backup
3. Dead letter queues — failed items go somewhere recoverable, not into the void
4. Real-time alert notifications — Slack, PagerDuty, email. Within seconds, not days
5. Graceful degradation — processing 9,800 of 10,000 records is infinitely better than processing zero

Autonoly has all 5 built into the workflow builder. Every node supports retry logic, error branching, and alert triggers. No custom code required.

Stop building for the happy path. Build for day 48.

Caption:
𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗜 𝗗𝗼𝗻’𝘁 𝗔𝗴𝗿𝗲𝗲 𝗪𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗛𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗔𝗿𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱 "𝗩𝗶𝗯𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴"
Lately, I've seen a lot of developers criticizing "vibe coding" and AI-assisted development as if it's somehow destroying software engineering.

I see it differently.

The problem isn't the tools.

The problem is how people use them.

A calculator didn't kill mathematics. GitHub didn't kill programming. Frameworks didn't kill software engineering. AI won't either.

What AI does is amplify the capabilities of the person using it.

If someone lacks understanding of architecture, databases, security, scalability, debugging, or software design principles, AI will simply help them make mistakes faster.

But for experienced developers who understand the fundamentals, AI becomes a force multiplier.

Over the last few months, I've shipped hundreds of projects ranging from SaaS platforms, digital products, business applications, web portals, mobile applications, dashboards, and desktop software. AI-assisted development didn't replace my expertise it accelerated execution.

Instead of spending hours on repetitive boilerplate code, I spend more time solving business problems, improving user experiences, refining architecture, and delivering products faster.

The reality is that clients don't pay for how many lines of code you write.

They pay for solutions.

They pay for products that work.

They pay for results.

The developers who will thrive in the coming years won't be the ones fighting AI. They'll be the ones learning how to combine technical expertise, product thinking, and AI tools to build better software faster than ever before.

AI is not replacing developers.

Developers who know how to leverage AI effectively are replacing those who refuse to adapt.

What's your take on AI-assisted development and vibe coding?

Caption:
Todo mês a mesma ansiedade: quanto vai vir na fatura esse mês?

Hora extra aqui, chamado fora do escopo ali, reunião que virou projeto, projeto que virou cobrança extra. O backoffice tradicional funciona assim — e você nunca sabe o número real até o boleto chegar.

A gente construiu um modelo diferente.

Fee fixo. Todo mês. Sem surpresa, sem negociação na última hora, sem "ah, mas isso não estava no contrato". Você sabe exatamente o que vai pagar antes de fechar o mês — e o que vai receber em troca.

Isso muda completamente a forma como um CFO ou diretor consegue planejar. Não é só uma questão de conforto. É previsibilidade de caixa. É poder olhar para o próximo trimestre sem uma variável descontrolada no meio do custo operacional.

Quando o backoffice vira um serviço gerenciado com escopo claro, agentes de IA executando o operacional e integrações rodando sem depender de pessoa física para cada tarefa manual, o custo deixa de ser imprevisível e passa a ser estratégico.

Previsível não significa barato. Significa controlável. E controle é o que separa operação de gestão. 🎯

Se o seu backoffice ainda funciona no modelo "vamos ver o que vem", talvez seja hora de repensar a estrutura toda.

#gestaofinanceira #automacaoempresarial #backoffice #financeirodigital #saas

Caption:
99% de SLA não é só número bonito em contrato. É a diferença entre um backoffice que você consegue cobrar e um que você torce pra funcionar.

A maioria das empresas opera no escuro. Tarefas críticas dependem de pessoas que ficam doentes, saem de férias, pedem demissão. E quando algo para, você só descobre depois que o estrago já foi feito. 🔍

Quando o operacional roda com agentes de IA, muda o jogo: cada processo tem tempo de execução medido, cada entrega tem log, cada falha gera alerta imediato. Não é promessa — é dado. É o mesmo princípio que nuvens enterprise usam pra garantir disponibilidade de infraestrutura, aplicado agora ao seu backoffice financeiro, fiscal e operacional.

CFOs estão começando a exigir isso. Não por modismo, mas porque finalmente é possível. Você consegue abrir um painel e ver: conciliação bancária executada em X minutos, relatório de fechamento entregue no prazo, integrações com ERP rodando sem intervenção humana.

Isso é gestão de verdade. Visibilidade real. Responsabilidade mensurável. 📊

Se o seu fornecedor de operações não consegue te mostrar o SLA dele, talvez o problema não seja a equipe — seja a falta de estrutura pra medir.

Most AI projects fail. Not because the technology breaks—because the business thinking breaks first.

𝗠𝗿. 𝗩𝗶𝗻𝗮𝘆 𝗕𝗮𝗯𝘂 𝗧𝗮𝗿𝗮𝗹𝗮, 𝗚𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗿𝗮𝗹 𝗦𝗲𝗰𝗿𝗲𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘆 & 𝗠𝗔𝗥𝗖𝗢𝗠 𝗼𝗳 𝗣𝗠𝗜𝗣𝗖𝗖, shared a pattern organizations repeat:
✗ Cool demo launched
✗ Stakeholders excited
✗ Pilot begins
✗ No adoption
✗ Project dies

𝗠𝗿. 𝗧𝗮𝗿𝗮𝗹𝗮 𝘀𝗽𝗼𝗸𝗲 𝗱𝗶𝗿𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗹𝘆 𝘁𝗼 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗲: Organizations ask the wrong question at the start. They ask "How can we use AI?" instead of "Should we use AI here at all?"

With 16+ years translating enterprise strategy into scaled execution, Mr. Tarala introduced CPMAI—Cognitive Project Management for AI. The message was clear:

𝗜𝘁'𝘀 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗺𝗽𝘁𝘀 𝗼𝗿 𝗺𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗹𝘀. 𝗜𝘁'𝘀 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲.

𝗛𝗲 𝘄𝗮𝗹𝗸𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗿𝗼𝘂𝗴𝗵 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗖𝗣𝗠𝗔𝗜 𝗟𝗶𝗳𝗲𝗰𝘆𝗰𝗹𝗲:
1️⃣ 𝗕𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗨𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 — Why? (Not "how impressive is the tech?")
2️⃣ 𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮 𝗨𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 — Do we have usable, trustworthy data?
3️⃣ 𝗠𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴 — Can AI actually solve this problem?
4️⃣ 𝗘𝘃𝗮𝗹𝘂𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 — Does it work well enough?
5️⃣ 𝗗𝗲𝗽𝗹𝗼𝘆𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 — Will people actually use it?
6️⃣ 𝗠𝗼𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗼𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 — Is it still creating measurable business value?

𝗠𝗿. 𝗧𝗮𝗿𝗮𝗹𝗮'𝘀 𝗚𝗼𝗹𝗱𝗲𝗻 𝗥𝘂𝗹𝗲:
AI Success = Business + Data + Technology + Adoption

He grounded it with a real example: PMIPCC is building an AI Member Assistant because 100 members ask the same 5 questions every week. The leverage point was obvious. The framework made it safe and scalable.

𝗛𝗶𝘀 𝗰𝗹𝗼𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗴𝗵𝘁:
Strategy without execution fails. Execution without intelligence stalls. Intelligence without governance scales the wrong things.

The room left understanding:
𝗧𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗸 𝗹𝗶𝗸𝗲 𝗮 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱𝗲𝗿, 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗮 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘀𝘂𝗺𝗲𝗿. 𝗕𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲. 𝗚𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗻 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝗽𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗯𝗹𝘆.

techniques that actually move the output:

03 few-shot examples — show 2-3 examples before your ask

05 negative prompting — "do not explain, just give the code"

the model is smart. your prompt is the bottleneck.

which one are you using? ↓

Caption:
Most companies waste hours every week on documents that AI could process automatically.

Mistral OCR 4 is not just another OCR tool. It returns structured data: where text sits on the page, what type of block it is, and a confidence score for each extraction. That is exactly what AI agents need to take reliable action on invoices, contracts, forms, and reports.

What this makes possible:

Invoices processed without per-supplier templates. Contracts reviewed in minutes instead of hours. Multilingual documents handled by a single pipeline across 170 languages. Company knowledge that is actually searchable with source citations. Compliance workflows with built-in audit trails.

The key design decision: use confidence thresholds as your workflow gate. High confidence extractions flow automatically. Low confidence ones go to human review. You get speed where accuracy is high and safety where it is not.

The companies building document automation infrastructure now will have a significant operational advantage as the space matures.

Een AI die zelf je scherm bedient, draait nu op het goedkope model.

Google bouwde computer use in Gemini 3.5 Flash. Tot nu toe was dat een apart, gespecialiseerd model. Nu zit het in de snelle, goedkopere Flash-laag.

Het verschil: zo'n agent krijgt geen nette API. Hij kijkt naar het scherm zoals jij dat doet, en klikt en typt zelf. Dus ook software zonder koppeling komt binnen bereik. Dat oude interne systeem, dat leveranciersportaal.

Dus: begin klein. Een afgebakende, niet-kritische taak. Bevestiging voor alles wat geld, data of klanten raakt. Logging en een kill-switch.

Author: armaraos

Caption:
Begint je AI-agent vaker fouten te maken? Dan denk je misschien dat prompt engineering de oplossing is. Maar het probleem zit eigenlijk ergens anders:

Er is een vaardigheid die veel meer impact heeft en waar nog weinig over gesproken wordt, namelijk context engineering.

De twee klinken gelijkaardig, maar ze spelen zich op een heel ander niveau af. 

➡ Prompt engineering gaat alleen over hoe je je prompt opstelt.

➡ Context engineering gaat over alles wat een AI-agent weet gedurende een volledige werksessie, en hoe je dat actief beheert.

Moderne AI-modellen hebben een context window van zo'n 200.000 tokens. Dat klinkt ruim, tot je ziet hoe snel die volloopt. Een agent die tegelijk je codebase analyseert, tools aanroept, gesprekshistoriek bijhoudt en instructies onthoudt, vult die ruimte sneller dan je denkt.

Veel developers zullen het gevoel herkennen. Je werkt al een tijdje met je agent, en code die hij een halfuur geleden nog correct schreef, zit nu vol fouten. Hij lijkt de instructies te vergeten die je aan het begin van de sessie meegaf. Dus pas je je prompt aan, maar het wordt er niet beter op.

Het voelt alsof de tool gewoon onbetrouwbaar is, maar eigenlijk zit de context window gewoon te vol om nog goed te kunnen redeneren.

Er is een drempel, ruwweg 40% van de context window, waarna de kwaliteit merkbaar daalt. Ontwikkelaars noemen dat de "Dumb Zone". De meeste mensen weten niet dat die drempel bestaat, en werken er daardoor de hele tijd tegenaan zonder te begrijpen wat er misgaat.

De oplossing zit in begrijpen wat er in die context window zit, wat eruit moet, en hoe je een agent structureert zodat hij gefocust blijft. Een andere prompt schrijven lost het probleem niet op.

We schreven er een uitgebreide blog over met concrete technieken om je context window goed te beheren. 

👉 Lees het op onze blog. Link in bio.

#ContextEngineering #AIAgents #AIDrivenDevelopment

Author: chatbot.summit

Caption:
Manual competitor tracking is a quiet productivity killer. Most growth teams waste hours every week bookmarking pricing pages, checking changelogs, and copy-pasting screenshots into stale spreadsheets. 

Here is how to automate the entire loop in under an hour using a simple no-code stack (Make + Clay + OpenAI):

1. Detection (Make): Set up a daily trigger to ping competitor change logs, pricing tables, or homepages.
2. Extraction (Clay): Fetch the updated HTML and compare it against the snapshot from the previous day.
3. Analysis (GPT-4o): Feed the raw HTML differences into an LLM with a highly specific prompt: "Ignore minor structural script changes. Extract actual changes in feature positioning, pricing plans, or messaging."
4. Delivery (Slack/Teams): Push clean, structured bullet-point summaries directly to your GTM channel.

Instead of manual browsing, your sales and product teams get market intelligence updates on autopilot. Live competitive awareness, zero manual scraping.

What workflows are you automating to keep tabs on your market?

Author: dann_paker_ai

Caption:
Your AI has six doors into your business. Most companies have only ever locked the front one.

When most people think about AI security, they think about keeping attackers out.

Firewalls.

Authentication.

Access controls.

The front door.

But AI systems don't operate through a single door.

They interact with users, consume data, connect to tools, store context, integrate with third-party systems, and influence business processes.

Each of those becomes a potential entry point.

Here are six doors every organization should be paying attention to:

1. The Prompt Door

Anyone who can interact with your AI can attempt to influence its behavior.

2. The Data Door

AI systems learn from and rely on the information they receive. Poor or manipulated data can create poor outcomes.

3. The Tool Door

Every API, database, email platform, or business system connected to AI expands its ability to take action.

4. The Memory Door

What your AI remembers can be just as important as what it knows. Retained context creates both value and risk.

5. The Integration Door

Third-party applications, plugins, and connectors often introduce risks organizations never evaluate.

6. The Human Door

Employees remain one of the largest attack surfaces. Shadow AI usage, unmanaged tools, and poor practices can bypass even the strongest technical controls.

The mistake many organizations make is securing the model while ignoring the ecosystem around it.

But attackers rarely target the strongest point

They look for the easiest door.

The question isn't: "Is our AI secure?"

The better question is: "Which of these six doors is currently open?"

Because AI security isn't about protecting a model.

It's about securing every pathway that can influence its decisions and actions.

📌 Save this post and use it as a checklist during your next AI security 
review.

Caption:
One of the biggest automation mistakes I see is jumping to the solution too early.

How much does a palletiser cost?

How much does a capping machine cost?

What's the budget?

They're all reasonable questions.

The problem is that they're often asked before we've fully understood what needs to be achieved.

What product?
What throughput?
What level of flexibility?
What labour challenges are we trying to solve?
What does success actually look like?

It's tempting to jump straight to solutions and budgets.

But that's where projects can start on the wrong foot.

The wrong assumptions get made.

The wrong expectations get set.

And sometimes the wrong solution gets pursued altogether.

The best automation projects I've been involved in all started the same way.

Not with a machine.

Not with a budget.

With a problem.

Understand the problem properly and the right solution usually becomes much easier to find.

#automation #manufacturing #engineering #industrialautomation #problemsolving #processevolution

Author: devops.kannadiga

Caption:
These are 9 DevOps mistakes that show up repeatedly in startups.

Some are expensive. Some are embarrassing. A few can take down entire systems.

1. One AWS account for everything
Dev, staging, and production in the same account creates unnecessary risk.

A dev misconfiguration can directly impact production or cost.

Separate accounts early.

2. No billing alerts
Without cost monitoring, cloud spend becomes invisible until it’s too late.

A single Auto Scaling misconfiguration once triggered 50 instances instead of 5 for days.

Result: a $12,000 bill.

3. Over-engineering before launch
Kubernetes, service meshes, and complex infrastructure before product-market fit.

Most early-stage systems fail from complexity, not lack of scale.

4. Hardcoded AWS credentials
Embedding credentials in code or repositories is a critical security risk.

Secrets should be managed properly from day one (not “later”).

5. Public SSH access (0.0.0.0/0 on port 22)
Exposing SSH to the internet guarantees exposure to automated brute-force attacks.

Session Manager or restricted access should be the default.

6. Deploying on Friday afternoons
Production changes close to weekends increase risk and slow response time if something breaks.

Deployment timing is part of reliability strategy.

7. No rollback strategy
Every deployment should answer one question:

How is this reverted in minutes if it breaks production?

Without that, deployments are high-risk events.

8. No monitoring before launch
Monitoring after an outage is reactive, not operational maturity.

CloudWatch alarms and health checks should exist before production traffic.

9. Single point of failure everywhere
Single EC2, single AZ, single NAT Gateway introduces avoidable downtime risk.

Systems should assume components will fail not behave perfectly.

Most production incidents are not caused by complex failures.

They come from basic infrastructure decisions made too early or too casually.

Strong DevOps practice is often just avoiding these fundamentals.

#devops #aws #cloudcomputing #sre #systemdesign

Caption:
Op zoek naar een BBL-stage waar je écht iets leert?

Denk jij bij een stage nog aan meekijken en simpele klusjes? Niet bij AWS. Wij zijn een erkend leerbedrijf, en dat betekent dat jij bij ons het vak écht leert. Gewoon in de praktijk.

Wat je bij ons gaat doen:

Caption:
Patch day.

Two words that can trigger anything from mild annoyance to full-blown panic depending on how old your infrastructure is.

Most teams don't intentionally neglect updates. They're busy shipping features, onboarding customers and fixing production issues. Updating third-party infrastructure components often ends up somewhere at the bottom of the backlog, right next to all those things everyone agrees are important but nobody has time for.

The problem is that technical debt compounds.

Maybe your Kubernetes version is a few releases behind. Your Helm charts haven't been updated in months. That Terraform provider still works alright, so why check it? Then eventually the day comes when you actually need to upgrade something and suddenly you're not upgrading one version. You're upgrading ten or more.

I've seen this happen more than once. What should have been a routine maintenance task turned into a project spanning several weeks because too many dependencies had drifted too far apart. The longer you delay, the more painful it becomes.

That's why I'm a big fan of continuous upgrades. Not because upgrading software is exciting. It isn't. But reviewing ten small changes every week is significantly easier than reviewing three hundred changes once per year.

Tools like Renovate help automate a large part of that process. Instead of manually checking every dependency, container image, Terraform provider or Helm chart, Renovate continuously scans for updates and creates pull requests automatically.

You still stay in control. Nothing gets deployed without review, but your team no longer has to remember what needs updating and when. The result is usually not only just better security, but also less operational risk. Because patch days stop being special events. They become part of normal operations.

How are you currently handling updates for third-party infrastructure components? Are you already using Renovate or are upgrades still mostly manual?

Caption:
⚡ Most data engineers use Spark every day — but freeze when asked: "What does the Driver actually do?"
Here's the truth nobody told you clearly:
Spark has a "Brain" and a "Muscle."
🧠 DRIVER = The Brain

→ Converts your code into a DAG (execution plan)

→ Schedules tasks across the cluster

→ Tracks which executor is alive and which crashed

→ Never processes a single row of data itself
💪 EXECUTOR = The Muscle

→ Actually runs your transformations & actions

→ Caches data in memory for speed

→ Sends heartbeats to the Driver every few seconds

→ Lives on worker nodes across the cluster
Here's what nobody talks about:
If your Driver dies → your entire job dies. It holds the SparkContext.

If one Executor dies → Spark just reruns that task elsewhere. No drama.
Knowing this one thing changes how you debug, tune, and architect every Spark application you'll ever write.
I wrote a deep-dive article that explains the whole Driver–Executor duo in a way that will actually stick.
📖 Link in bio → Medium: Spark Driver vs Executor

Caption:
🔷 Delta Tables — The Complete Breakdown (Save this 🔖)

If you’re working with data lakes and you’re NOT using Delta Tables yet, you’re flying blind. No rollbacks. No history. No reliable upserts. Just vibes and prayer when something breaks at 2am.

We broke down everything you need to know across 10 slides 👇

✅ What Delta Tables actually are
✅ The architecture behind the magic (_delta_log explained)
✅ ACID transactions — why they matter more than you think
✅ Time Travel — query your data from ANY point in history
✅ MERGE — the upsert operation that runs production pipelines
✅ Schema enforcement vs evolution
✅ Delta vs raw Parquet — full comparison

Caption:
📅 Dia 08 de 30 do Desafio de dados Databricks Professional

✨ Auto Loader Master: Schema Evolution e Detecção de Drift

​Abrindo a segunda semana do desafio com o pé no acelerador em Ingestão Escalável e Streaming! Hoje o foco total foi dominar o Auto Loader e aprender a lidar com uma das maiores dores de cabeça na engenharia de dados: a mudança repentina na estrutura dos arquivos que chegam no Data Lake.

​📌 O Poder do Auto Loader (cloudFiles)
​Ingestão Incremental Eficiente: Ele descobre novos arquivos assim que eles chegam no S3/ADLS de forma absurdamente eficiente, usando notificação de arquivos ou listagem otimizada, sem precisar reprocessar o que já passou.

​Schema Evolution (Evolução de Esquema): Em vez de o pipeline quebrar no meio da madrugada porque uma nova coluna surgiu, o Auto Loader consegue detectar a mudança e evoluir o schema automaticamente (cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode), adaptando a tabela Delta sem intervenção manual.

​Detecção de Schema Drift com _rescued_data: Se uma coluna nova aparecer ou um tipo de dado mudar drasticamente, o Auto Loader joga essa informação para uma coluna especial de "dados resgatados". Isso garante que o pipeline continue rodando e nenhum dado seja perdido enquanto você analisa a mudança.

​✨ Reflexão Diária
No mundo real, os dados são vivos e mudam o tempo todo. Um pipeline verdadeiramente resiliente não é aquele que assume que o dado sempre virá perfeito, mas sim aquele que sabe se adaptar ao "drift" de forma inteligente e segura.

Caption:
🚀 30 Days of Azure Data Engineering | Day 4

Today's topic: Azure Integration Runtime (IR) — one of the most important concepts in Azure Data Factory.

Many engineers learn pipelines, datasets, and linked services, but understanding Integration Runtime helps explain how data actually moves between systems.

Key takeaway:

🔹 Azure IR → Cloud-to-Cloud Data Movement
🔹 Self-Hosted IR → On-Premises to Cloud Connectivity
🔹 Azure SSIS IR → Lift-and-Shift SSIS Workloads

Think of IR as the execution engine that powers data movement and activity execution inside Azure Data Factory.

If you've worked with ADF in production, chances are you've used one of these IR types.

Caption:

Caption:
Why the data system your company built for basic reporting is quietly failing your AI. 🛑🖥️

Most corporations organize their modern data lakes using a pattern called the "Medallion Architecture," which filters raw info through three separate steps (Bronze, Silver, and Gold). While this is great for basic charts and graphs, it creates a massive blind spot for advanced AI features like search bots and automated assistants.

When an AI model hallucinated or retrieves the wrong document piece, your developers need to know exactly where and when that individual row of data was corrupted. Traditional systems can only track large groups of tables, making finding a single broken record look like searching for a needle in a haystack.

Upgrading to Apache Iceberg v3 introduces native Row Lineage. This smart update tags every single row with its own digital passport (_row_id), allowing your engineers to instantly query the life history of any data record with 100% precision.

Stop trying to power tomorrow's AI on yesterday's blueprints. Learn how to architect elite, crystal-clear enterprise data platforms at www.mcal.in.

Caption:
If AI ROI is on your boardroom agenda this year, this is worth five minutes of your time!
 
In his latest Forbes article, Nachiket Deshpande explores what many enterprises are beginning to discover: the challenge isn't the AI model, it's the context.
 
Agentic AI can reason. It can automate. It can execute.
 
But without enterprise #context, your business rules, processes, systems, institutional knowledge, and decision frameworks, even the most capable AI agents struggle to deliver measurable value. They generate outputs, not outcomes.
 
The next wave of competitive advantage won't come from deploying more AI agents. It will come from giving those agents the context they need to make business-aware decisions.
 
A timely perspective for every business leader evaluating AI ROI.

Stop overthinking your interest groups and start looking at your visuals.

The days of micro-targeting through complex settings are fading. Today, your ad creative is actually your new targeting setting.

Here is how it works in plain English:

Modern algorithms are incredibly smart. They do not just show your ad to who you tell them to; they show it to people who actually stop and look at it.

Your "Hook" acts as a filter. It catches the attention of your ideal customer while others keep scrolling.

Your "Message" acts as a magnet. It builds interest with the right people, telling the algorithm: "Find more people like this."

Instead of spending hours tweaking audience segments, spend that time crafting a message that resonates. If your creative is right, the algorithm will find your people.

Caption:
Most businesses spend hours on their marketing and about thirty seconds on the one part that actually drives results.

The call to action.

It's the only line in your email, ad, or social post that asks someone to do something. Everything before it is just build-up. And yet "click here" or "find out more" gets added at the last minute and nobody questions it.

A weak CTA doesn't announce itself. It just quietly costs you conversions, month after month, while everything else looks fine.

Rich Brown (QBD Head of Lead Generation) has written a full breakdown of what makes a CTA actually work, and the mistakes that kill results before anyone even reads them.
https://www.qbd.co.uk/how-to-fix-your-landing-pages/

Author: finsburymedia

Caption:
CASE STUDY: ONLINE COACH

📈 An online coach had a problem many businesses secretly struggle with.

People were visiting her website every day.

Traffic wasn't the issue.

The real question was:

**"If people are visiting my website, why aren't they buying?"**

Hundreds of visitors would come.

They would read about her programs.

Browse her services.

Then leave.

No enquiry.

No purchase.

No follow-up.

Then she realized something important:

**Most people don't buy the first time they see your offer.**

People need time.

People need trust.

People need reminders.

So instead of trying to sell immediately, she changed her strategy.

She created a simple **5-day email nurture sequence.**

For five days, she stayed connected with her audience.

📩 Day 1: Welcome email and her story

📩 Day 2: Valuable tips and practical advice

📩 Day 3: Client success stories and testimonials

📩 Day 4: Solutions to common objections

📩 Day 5: A clear offer with a call to action

She stopped chasing customers.

She started building relationships.

The results after just 30 days were impressive:

📧 48% email open rate

📈 27% increase in sales

The biggest lesson?

💡 **Follow-up creates conversions.**

Many businesses lose customers not because their product is bad, but because they stop the conversation too early.

Remember this:

❌ Website visitors are not lost opportunities.

❌ People rarely buy the first time they see you.

✅ Consistent follow-up builds trust.

✅ Trust creates sales.

The fortune is often in the follow-up.

👇 Be honest: Are you following up with your potential customers?

Comment **YES** or **NO** below.

💾 Save this post and share it with a business owner who needs this reminder.

#EmailMarketing #DigitalMarketing #LeadNurturing #BusinessGrowth #ContentMarketing #MarketingTips #OnlineBusiness #Entrepreneurship

Author: fleurasselman.be

Caption:
The game changed. The foundation didn't.

Search used to be about ranking on Google. Now it's about being trusted by AI.

But here's what most marketers are getting wrong: they're treating GEO (Generative Engine Optimisation) and SEO as a choice — pick one, skip the other.

That's not how it works.

→ GEO without SEO means AI has no reason to trust you. No domain authority, no structured content, no backlink credibility — AI models skip right past you when forming answers.

→ SEO without GEO means you rank on Google, but you're invisible when someone asks ChatGPT, Perplexity, or Gemini a question your business should be answering.

You don't get to choose one layer. Both need to run simultaneously, or you're already losing ground to competitors who figured this out.

The brands winning in AI-era search built their SEO foundation first — then layered GEO on top: structured answers, cited content, E-E-A-T signals, semantic clarity.

The question isn't SEO or GEO.

It's: are you running both — and are they talking to each other?

#SEO #GEO #AIMarketing #SearchStrategy #GenerativeSearch

Caption:
Investor thua 18 lần vẫn thắng — bạn chỉ có 1 ván, vậy bạn đa dạng hóa rủi ro kiểu gì? 🎯

Đây là bất đối xứng lớn nhất giữa founder và investor mà ít ai chịu ngồi xuống phân tích thẳng thắn.

Một VC có thể rót tiền vào 20 startup cùng lúc. Họ biết trước rằng 18 cái sẽ chết — và họ vẫn ngủ ngon vì chỉ cần 1 đến 2 cái thắng lớn là cả portfolio có lãi. 

...

Đó là đặc quyền của người chơi với portfolio. 

Nhưng founder không có đặc quyền đó. Bạn chỉ có 1 công ty duy nhất. 

Nếu nó chết, không có portfolio nào bù lại. Không có vòng tiếp theo để cân bằng. 

Vậy founder "đa dạng hóa rủi ro" bằng cách nào khi không thể đầu tư dàn trải như investor?

Sau hơn 14 năm coaching và trực tiếp tham gia BOD tại VuaCua, Nina Ecom Center, VCO Group, HOPE Corp, tôi quan sát thấy 3 lớp bảo vệ mà phần lớn founder không chủ động xây — và thường chỉ nhận ra mình thiếu khi đã quá muộn.

1)Lớp đầu tiên là đa dạng dòng doanh thu. 💰

Khi 80% doanh thu đến từ 1 sản phẩm duy nhất hoặc 1 khách hàng duy nhất, bạn không đang kinh doanh — bạn đang đặt cược. 

Nguyên tắc quan trọng ở đây là dòng doanh thu mới phải tận dụng được năng lực cốt lõi sẵn có, không cần build lại từ đầu. 

Đây là bộ đệm, không phải sự phân tán. 

Mở thêm revenue stream mà không liên quan gì đến core competency thực ra còn nguy hiểm hơn — vì nó chia cắt sự tập trung mà không tạo ra bảo vệ thực sự.

2)Lớp thứ hai là đa dạng kênh phân phối. 📊

Caption:
MJF a Riccardo Donadon: un riconoscimento a chi trasforma l'innovazione in valore per la comunità.
Roberto Limitone, Governatore del Distretto 108Ta3, ha conferito la Melvin Jones Fellow a Riccardo Donadon, Amministratore Delegato e fondatore di H-FARM.
Un riconoscimento che premia non solo una brillante carriera imprenditoriale, capace di fare di H-FARM uno dei principali ecosistemi europei dedicati all'innovazione, all'economia digitale e alla formazione, ma anche una visione che pone la tecnologia al servizio delle persone.
Attraverso il sostegno alla nascita di start-up innovative e di progetti ad alto impatto sociale, Riccardo Donadon ha saputo promuovere modelli di sviluppo fondati sulla solidarietà, sull'inclusione e sulla creazione di nuove opportunità per le giovani generazioni e per le comunità.
Congratulazioni a Riccardo Donadon per questo prestigioso riconoscimento.

Author: 108ta3

Caption:
Hey fam!

Last night, we joined AI Tinkerers Hong Kong & GBA at Dialogue in the Dark, a great evening about building AI that genuinely enables. 🤖

The night opened the way it should: with the people AI is meant to serve. Visually impaired hosts from Dialogue in the Dark shared how AI is already reshaping their daily lives and, just as importantly, the real-world UX problems they still need engineers to solve. A grounding reminder that accessibility isn’t a feature you bolt on at the end; it’s the brief. 🙂

From there, the demos. Three teams shared technical work spanning two accessibility projects and a live translation app - the kind of “does it actually work in the wild?” building we love to see. AWS & Kiro also dropped in for a session (and kept the room fed 🙏).

What struck us most was the crowd: deeply technical, hands-on, the kind of people training their own models and hacking on hard problems for the right reasons. Exactly the energy we really love being around! 😉

At OAX Foundation, this is the intersection we care about: #AI that extends human capability and reaches the people who need it most. It’s also why we back projects like @gmasiahq mapping the people, ideas, and events moving Asia’s #AI scene forward. Nights like this are a big part of that story. 🥰

Huge thanks to Dialogue in the Dark for hosting, and to AI Tinkerers HK & GBA for convening such a thoughtful room. More of this, please! 🥂

#tech #startup #hongkong

Caption:
You know when you type a question into your LLM (let's say Claude), and it says...thinking...

Or, it gives you the impression it's thinking.

Well, it's not.

Your AI is a predication engine. It doesn't have thoughts, or feelings for that matter. It has lots and lots of data and it uses that to predict the most likely answer.

This means, it doesn't always get it right, which is why fact checking is so important.

Being honest, have you started to rely on AI a bit like it's a friend? Seriously no judgement! 😊 It's easy to do, that's what tech companies are trying to achieve in their interfaces and responses.

Try this instead. Use your LLM to help challenge you. To broaden your cognitive skills, not lessen them. Try sparring with your AI model on a subject you know about and whilst doing so, remember, it's a prediction engine, it's not really thinking and you can't hurt its feelings.

#AISolutions #AIConsultant #AIStrategy #AutomationExpert #DigitalTransformation

Caption:
AI can do a lot, but it can't carry responsibility for your decisions.

That's why the smartest teams are building AI and human judgement side by side, on purpose.

AI is great at the heavy lifting: analysing data, spotting patterns, and drafting options in seconds.

Humans are great at the trust work: nuance, empathy, ethical reasoning, and accountability.

So before you roll out another tool, get clear on roles.

What will AI automate, what will it advise on, and what must a person approve every time?
Next, build a workflow where AI outputs come with a review moment, not an auto-send button.

If the decision affects people, money, safety, or privacy, add stronger oversight and clear escalation paths.
Keep the loop tight so your AI improves responsibly: create, deploy, measure, review, refine, redeploy.
And reinvest the time you save into the skills that compound, like critical thinking, communication, and great leadership.

Our principle at Maybe* is simple: let AI banish repetitive tasks so your humans can shine at strategy and creativity.
If you want a practical starting point, book a call with a Maybe* expert and we'll help you choose a safe pilot and scale with confidence. Start with one task. Head to our bio to learn more.
